data.title: Kimbell Royalty Partners: A Double-Digit Yield And Exceptional Business Model For Long-Term Investors
data.description:
Thesis

The investment choice of Kimbell Royalty Partners (KRP) is most suitable for investors who plan to hold their shares for the long term.

The company operates a successful business model while maintaining strong financial fundamentals and a dividend policy that benefits shareholders.

The stock faces limited potential for upward movement because its market capitalization stands at $1.6B and royalties sector growth remains moderate.

The company maintains solid financials which indicate its high dividend (14% yield, $1.88 FWD) will remain secure even if it experiences a minor reduction in the future.

The May 7 acquisition of 50% Series A Preferred Shares initially reduced distributable cash flow, but it will boost future redemption value and decrease medium- to long-term capital costs while demonstrating management's dedication to shareholder value.

The combination of operational excellence with management commitment to shareholder value creates investor and shareholder confidence which drives solid long-term investment choices.

Stock Overview

The stock has been in a long-term downward sideways trend. Investors seem to ignore the company’s effort of demonstrating its actual value, through consistent solid financial results.

Unfortunately, as I will analyze below, the mediocre expectations for further growth or even for strong positive shifts act as an obstacle on investor demand for the stock.

The issue seems to lie in the weak expectations for the stock’s outperformance and not in the viability of the Company itself.

The chart shows that the $11.50–$11.70 range was the lower limit of the decline and the stock has reacted strongly, with gains of more than 20% from its April lows.

At this point, 14.50 is the first nearby resistance, with 16–16.20 being the first target on the upside, and a critical long-term barrier, +23% from current levels.

It is important to observe the significant increase in trading activity in recent weeks, which strengthens the value of 11.50, setting the foundation for a possible upward move.

In early 2025, the company completed a pricing for its increased public offering of 10 million common units at $14.90 each, raising total gross proceeds of $149.0 million.

In the statistics, the stock has lost nearly 5 dollars from the listing price (summer 2017, $18), with losses of around 27%. However, the shareholders are not at a loss, since over 8 years of holding they have earned, through dividends, almost 46%.

Strong dividend yield

From the above, it is obvious that KRP will be used for now as a vehicle of dividend yield and not for the achievement of excess profit from the stock. From my perspective, however, the stock seems “cheap”, especially for those who are patient and invest in long-term value, and it has more chances to move upward over time, rather than to move downward.

With napkin math, it is more likely to be valued at $20 (+50%) over time than at $6.5 (-50%) but as I said above, considering the company’s ability to generate a solid dividend as more important, the chance that it will not cause me a loss in the long run is very high, while I will be enjoying a decent dividend.

As every experienced investor can understand, more than the strong dividend and the consistency with which the company distributes it, it is the behavior of the management toward the shareholders.

Beyond its ability to manage the company, it seems extremely friendly toward shareholders, in the sense that every action is taken without harming their interests.

Like, for example, the recent prudent Buyback of Series A Preferred Shares, which we will see below.

In any case, a few days ago it paid $0.47 (Record Date May 20, 2025, Payment Date May 28, 2025). This means roughly a 14% dividend, although it must be strongly emphasized in advance that the company does not distribute a fixed dividend, but one tied to prevailing conditions.

Also, the dividend was increased by 17.5% from Q4 2024.

Kimbell Royalty Partners estimates that about 70% of the distribution for the first quarter of 2025 will not be considered a dividend for U.S. federal income tax purposes, but a non-taxable return of capital that reduces each unitholder’s cost basis.

This reduction in the tax basis means that, upon selling the common units, the unitholder may face a higher capital gain or a smaller capital loss.

Also, the remaining 30% of the distribution, which is treated as a dividend for tax purposes, will be classified as a “qualified dividend” and therefore will be taxed at a rate of 0%, 15%, or 20%, depending on the income level and the tax status of the unitholder for 2025.

What makes this especially noteworthy for investors is that Kimbell is not a typical MLP (Master Limited Partnership) or an RT (Royalty Trust). Instead, it functions as a PTP (Publicly Traded Partnership) that has chosen to be taxed as a regular corporation, a structure similar to that of Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A) and other entities designed with tax efficiency in mind.

Partial Redemption of Preferred Units

For the next quarters, it is very difficult for the dividend to remain at $0.47, mainly due to the reduction of distributable cash flows.

Although it “paid” $182M (162,500 Series A Preferred Units), this move reflects management’s effort to further reduce capital costs.

Following this redemption and the anticipated repayment using the remaining 25% of Q1 2025 cash available for distribution, Kimbell estimates to have a debt under its secured credit line of $462.1 million. The company expects its net debt to trailing twelve months consolidated Adjusted EBITDA for Q1 2025 to be about 1.5 times, while maintaining compliance with all financial covenants of its secured credit facility.

Another interesting issue is that the company insists on a payout ratio of 75% of cash available for distribution, while the financial figures for the next quarters do not appear to be threatened.

Activity on acreage remains strong, with 90 Active Rigs Drilling, accounting for a 16% share of the U.S. Land Rig Count. According to the company, net income stood at $25.9 million. It also posted its highest consolidated adjusted EBITDA and cash available for distribution to date. The daily production run-rate for Q1 2025 hit 25,841 Boe/d (6:1), factoring in a full quarter of acquired output, exceeding the midpoint of its guidance.

Therefore, even if the “gap” created by the redemption is not filled, it is hard for anyone to predict that future dividends will not be satisfactory. Also, it is reiterated that the redemption was made with the purpose of reducing the cost of capital, and thus for the company to achieve even better results.

Finally, the borrowing base and total commitments on the credit line increased from $550 million to $625 million, so after the buyback and debt repayment with 25% of the distributable cash flows from Q1 2025, Kimbell estimates that the remaining debt on the credit line stands at approximately $462 million, and the utilization of the credit line is now about 74%.

Solid financials and an exceptional business model

I mentioned at the beginning that the sector of royalties has moderate growth prospects. To be clear, this does not mean that companies like KRP face problems but simply that they lag behind the prospects of more aggressive sectors, such as software, AI, etc.

In fact, this is good for investors who look for more “stable” options, a bit riskier than traditional bonds, who avoid large fluctuations but naturally give up on outsized returns.

KRP is one of those that continuously grows (and likely will continue) and regardless of the pace, the chances of facing serious problems are very low.

Thus, it gives confidence to those who choose it for investments and high dividends.

For example, the company has grown in the best way over the last decade, as can be seen from the revenues.

The company has a great ability of converting revenue into profit through its net income margin of 8.6%, which matches sector averages with its EBITDA margin reaching 75% (2.3 times higher than the sector average).

Kimbell Royalty operates as a leading company from Fort Worth, Texas that specializes in acquiring and managing mineral rights and oil and gas lease interests throughout the United States.

Since its establishment in 1998, Kimbell has evolved into one of the biggest mineral rights owners in the United States through its acquisition of more than 17 million gross acres across 28 states and its ownership of 131,000 gross wells, of which 52,000 are located in the Permian Basin.

The business model of Kimbell involves purchasing high-quality mineral rights with long-term potential and low decline rates to generate stable cash flows for its investors. Through its non-production business model Kimbell avoids operating costs and capital spending because third parties develop its leases at no expense to the company.

Risks

The stock valuation represents a major constraint for this investment. The stock price exceeds 18 times earnings, while the sector average stands at 11.3 times and enterprise value equals 8 times EBITDA.

The corporate structure of Kimbell Royalty Partners provides tax efficiency and stability, yet this does not guarantee the stock will be immune to market volatility. The stock price experiences fluctuations because of changes in oil and gas markets and insider trading activities and general macroeconomic conditions.

The features of Kimbell that attract investors include stable cash flows and high dividend payouts, but investors need to exercise caution due to its small market capitalization of $1.8 billion and limited growth potential.

Conclusion

The company is an excellent opportunity for investors who are attracted by long-term investments.

The company's particular business model in combination with its robust financial results ensures its value stability.

The dividend payment of 14% is satisfactory, and the analysis shows it will not deteriorate in the future, even though the final amount may experience minor fluctuations.

The stock should be included in income portfolios that focus on stocks with promising growth potential. Thus, I would say that a “BUY” recommendation is fully justified.

data.title: Vicor Corporation: Innovation Alone Won't Pay The Bills
data.description:
Vicor Corporation (NASDAQ:VICR) offers an interesting story. It has advanced technology that places it at the heart of the current trend of AI and vehicle electrification, etc. The company's potential seems enormous as it promises superior power and efficiency, and these are important factors for AI applications that are hungry for energy. However, the great technology alone doesn't guarantee success.

Although Vicor has advanced technology, the company works in an intensive capital and low-margin sector, as its capability for expanding and keeping customers are critical factors. The company's reliance on a limited number of large customers increases its fragility, as losing a single design like Nvidia H100 led to a significant loss of its market share to competitors such as MPS. This reliance limits the company's pricing power and weakens its revenues, especially in cyclical sectors such as semiconductors and vehicles. Considering this, besides the recent weak results. In my opinion, it makes sense to say that the risks outweigh the benefits.

Business Model

Vicor specializes in designing, manufacturing, and marketing power modules and components. The company's core strength is in its architecture called "Factorized Power Architecture" which allows it to separate the regulation, conversion, and isolation functions, resulting in higher power and efficiency compared to traditional approaches. Vicor's main products include DC converters, Voltage regulators, and complete power modules that serve a variety of markets.

The company targets the following sectors:

Enterprise & High-Performance Computing (HPC): this is currently the main driver for growth, especially with the AI applications boom, which require huge amounts of power for GPUs. Vicor's solutions are ideal for supplying the necessary power to these accelerators directly at the point of load, reducing the distribution losses and improving overall performance.

Industry: Vicor offers apps ranging from industrial automation to testing and measurement. It provides power modules for use in control systems and robots, which require highly efficient power sources.

Automotive: Vicor also provides its solutions for electric vehicles and autonomous driving, as the need is growing for high-density power solutions and the company sees it as a long-term growth opportunity.

Vicor provides a competitive advantage represented in delivering a better performance than its competitors, However, this advantage comes at a higher price, making it vulnerable to competitors with good enough and less expensive solutions.

Financial Results

In the first quarter of 2025, Vicor's results demonstrated a complex picture. The revenue declined to $94m, and it had EPS of $0.06 below market expectations, causing a sharp decline in the stock price. However, there is some positive news, the most notable was the revenue growth of 12% YoY along with a turnaround in net income. In contrast, the revenues declined 2.3% QoQ, the gross margin fell significantly to 47.2%, and the operating margin even turned negative, reflecting clear cost pressures.

The overall performance was mixed, as the advanced products did well, while Brick declined; partly due to the company's transition to a new ERP and increased R&D capex. These may be legitimate reasons, but at the same time reflect poor management planning.

On the legal front, the company achieved an important victory, after the USITC confirmed that some competitors infringing its patents. However, awarding a license to Foxconn raised questions about the effectiveness of this victory. Nevertheless, the royalty growth of 31.6%, providing a financial boost.

Vicor aims to meet the growing demand for AI power solutions, launching new DCM converters and developing the second generation of AI-VPD applications. Vicor's revenues have grown at a CAGR of 6.5% over the past 5 years, which is a modest number for a tech company. Additionally, The company's advanced products started to show some progress driven by its AI modules, but this progress hasn't been enough to offset the 2024 downturn.

The Gross margin has improved in recent years; however, it is still far from its 65% target. Given that, in addition to the sharp decline in the first quarter, Vicor's ability to reach this goal seems complex.

As for operating profits, it's no less disturbing, there is a lot of volatility, but The most worrying point is in the free cash flow; the company didn't generate any in the past 5 years, raising questions about its ability to fund its operations. However, it has a strong balance sheet with $296m and low debt, which is a bright spot in the clouds.

Competition

Vicor plays in a crowded market with direct and indirect competitors such as Monolithic Power Systems and Texas Instruments. The company has a number of patents and advantages that are trying to defend it. However, it took some blows; the most notable loss was the Nvidia H100 design in 2023 to its rival MPS. It was a significant loss and raised questions about the company's stability in the future. Even Vicor's promising technologies like VPD and Chipset, which were considered super technology, began to fade and competitors like MPS, ADI and others caught or even surpassed it, particularly in terms of implementation and economic efficiency.

Valuation

The company didn't provide guidance for 2025 due to uncertainties. This represents an uncomfortable situation for investors. However, I will try to estimate a valuation based on their recent results.

So in 2024, it had an EBITDA of $37M. At the same time, it has an FWD EV/EBITDA of approximately 27x. With that in mind and after adding cash of 296m, the equity value comes to $1295M, or $28.6 per share (shares outstanding at 45.2M).

Risks

Vicor has big ambitions, but it faces some significant competitive and operational risks that threaten its goal, as its loss of key designs to another rival like MPS raises questions about management efficiency and its ability to keep large clients. Therefore, its superiority in the market isn't guaranteed. Furthermore, Vicor has very volatile margins, which increases its sensitivity to any downturn or execution failures.

The company has a limited number of large customers and if one of Vicor's top three customers decided to delay his production cycle or transition to another competitor, the company's revenue could decline by 10% or even 20%, which is currently a non-reflected risk in stock valuation.

In terms of dispute gains, it's still threatened by additional costs or uncertain appeals.

Although Vicor has significant risks, there could be positive scenarios. If the company succeeded in acquiring a key AI design, like contracting with AMD or Intel, if it developed the second generation of AI-VPD successfully, or it exceeded market expectations due to patent litigation. These factors could improve earnings and reflect positively on market sentiment

With these risks in mind, the bet on Vicor becomes a bet on the management's ability to break the risk cycle.

Conclusion

Vicor has a beautiful story, but for the story to be convincing, it needs numbers to support it. This doesn't seem to be happening yet. Plus, the company has fierce competition and faces rising execution challenges. This is evident in the company's results and management's doubts about future guidance. Considering this along with the overvaluation, I believe it's fair to give it a "Sell" rating.

data.title: Olaplex Holdings: Strong Buy On EPS Rebound Driven By Margin Recovery
data.description:
Margin Recovery Unlocked; Strong Buy on EPS Rebound

We initiate on Olaplex Holdings, Inc. (OLPX) with a Strong Buy & $4 PT. Olaplex Holdings, Inc. manufactures and sells proprietary hair care products distributed to salons, retailers, and consumers across channels. What we like: Our thesis is a sharp, above-consensus EPS rebound: Our $0.11 FY25E & $0.15 FY26E vs The Street's $0.07, anchored in 'Designed to Defy' campaign success (>1bn press impressions, +5 SOV) and specialty retail sell-through.

The Street is missing accelerating premium SKU sell-through and $20mn annual interest expense savings, which will drive leverage and earnings power above consensus. Our $4 PT is based on 28.4x FY26E P/E, a disciplined multiple compression from OLPX's 71.4x today, balancing current margin pressures and growing cyclicality/interest relief signals. We see risk in premium category demand and competitor spend, but The Street models are anchored in backward trends, missing the inflection under way. Bottom line: with category leadership, innovation track record, and earnings trajectory ahead of consensus, OLPX offers attractive asymmetric R/R at current levels.

$300mn Debt Repayment: Unfolding EPS Inflection Point

Olaplex's voluntary $300mn debt repayment, executed at par on May 1, 2025 (with no prepayment penalties or fees), is a transformational and underappreciated catalyst for margin expansion and EPS inflection. Here's where the story crystallizes for equity investors: With management guiding to a ~$20mn annual interest expense savings, this is a recurring benefit flowing through to the bottom line as structural deleverage, not one-off cost savings.

In FQ1'25, net income was $0.5mn on $13.7mn reported interest expense, with interest expense multiple times operating income. By reducing the debt balance by $300mn (and still having >$280mn liquidity remaining), OLPX materially de-risks its balance sheet and lowers the breakeven point for future earnings growth. The financial leverage is immense: Every dollar of interest savings translates directly into operating leverage, freeing up cash for reinvestment and/or shareholder returns.

The Street models are often anchored to compressed margins, but few fully bake in the scale and permanence of these interest savings, which shifts the earnings base materially. It's a reset of the earnings power with a tangible, recurring benefit baked in far before consensus. In our view, this substantial interest expense reduction is the key driver for the outsized EPS inflection we model in. It's a structural lever that makes OLPX R/R compelling.

Specialty Retail Channel Outperformance: The Unappreciated Source of Mkt Share & Earnings Upside

The market opportunity becomes clear as we focus on Olaplex's specialty retail channel, which we see as the company's strongest near-term growth lever. In the last quarter, specialty retail sales accelerated +12% y/y, a remarkable feat that not only outpaced internal expectations but also surprised The Street. Where the rubber meets the road: behind the headline number, management highlighted strong, accelerating sell-through of premium SKUs, fueled by the "Designed to Defy" brand campaign and a renewed focus on strategic retailer partnerships. We believe channel performance is outpacing beauty industry trends and validating Olaplex's pricing power & innovation credentials.

What competitors can't replicate is the virtuous flywheel at play: enhanced sell-through in specialty retail doesn't just drive top-line growth; it also liberates shelf space, encourages retailer support, and amplifies brand mindshare among high-intent consumers. The logical outcome is twofold, not only is Olaplex gaining incremental market share at the expense of prestige-focused peers, but each incremental point of specialty retail sales has a ripple effect on margin recovery and bottom-line performance, given the channel's historically superior gross margin profile.

This channel outperformance, not fully appreciated by consensus models focused on recent channel destocking, is the kind of sustainable beat that drives a sharp, above-consensus EPS rebound. For investors, this channel dynamic is a structural tailwind that makes OLPX's R/R compelling-and underlies our strong buy conviction.

Olaplex: Above-Consensus EPS Recovery on Sell-Through/Margin Lever Acceleration

We model Olaplex EPS of $0.11/$0.15 in FY25E/FY26E, above The Street consensus ($0.07/$0.07), based on rapid brand-campaign innovation-cycle activation and quantifiable interest cost tailwinds. Our estimates differ from consensus, which extrapolates Q1 margin softness and assumes muted sell-through.

Source: FMP FY25 FY26
Estimate # Analysts Estimate # Analysts
Revenue $420.51M 6 $433.02M 8
EBITDA $213.81M 6 $220.18M 8
Net Income $43.51M 6 $53.75M 8
EPS $0.07 8 $0.07 5
SG&A Expenses $105.73M 6 $108.87M 8

We see evidence to the contrary, with the "Designed to Defy" campaign delivering over 1bn press impressions and +5 pts SOV growth, outcomes that historically drive robust Q2/Q3 sell-through and repeat purchasing. Specialty-retail strength, coupled with sell-through acceleration of new premium SKUs (No. 4-5 Fine, scalp treatment), sets up channel and MS gains vs. The Street models, which are anchored to flat sales/declining margin.

We also include $20mn of annualized interest savings from debt paydown, a margin lever unappreciated by The Street. While we acknowledge risks around premium consumer demand/competitive spend, we believe current volume/brand health/margin trend lines reinforce our view of a sharp earnings rebound. For clarity, while our EPS estimates are above The Street, we have a Strong Buy rating as we continue to monitor the robust multiple and repeat demand durability.

Valuation

Our $4 PT is based on 28.4x forward P/E multiple applied to our FY26E EPS of $0.15, implying 222% upside. The multiple represents a modest compression from current 71.4x (100th pctile of its own history and 98th versus peers) but still represents a premium to historical median (24.6x) and peer median (16.3x) as we believe our sharp above-consensus earnings snapback - driven by accelerating sell-through and $20mn in annualized interest expense relief - is the appropriate context for valuation.

We believe this snapback, with the early impact of the "Designed to Defy" campaign and specialty retail outperformance (+12% Q1), puts OLPX on track for 36% FY26E earnings growth. This is unlike consensus, which is anchored to near-term margin softness and category headwinds. Our implied multiple balances a recognition of ongoing margin pressure with clear signals of operating leverage and innovation led recovery, which deliberately compresses the risk premium without overlooking OLPX's differentiated brand platform.

Importantly, while The Street is fixated on headline valuation compression, we believe the alpha lies with the underappreciated earnings power of structurally lower interest expense, an inflection that is near invisible to sell-side consensus. Fundamentally, we find this valuation based on asymmetric R/R appropriate, both fairly acknowledging near-term uncertainty and the durable drivers of margin and earnings leverage.

Risks to our Strong Buy thesis

While our Strong Buy thesis on Olaplex relies on outsized EPS recovery, accelerating specialty retail [SS] sell-thru and robust margin leverage, we see credible risks to the outlook that could derail or at least delay this trajectory. Most notably, the degree of current professional (Pro down -27% y/y) and Direct-to-Consumer pressure, as well as continued international softness, introduces the possibility that premium haircare demand will not fully rebound to our modeled trajectory, particularly if consumer wallet tightening and/or competitive encroachment accelerates further.

Additionally, while the recent impact of "Designed to Defy" and "Bonds and Beyond" marketing is measurable in SS top-line inflection, a failure to translate brand investment into sustained volume recovery and repeat-buy rates, or the return of negative hair-loss litigation headlines could mute the planned margin inflection and reduce valuation catch-up.

The initial Q1 SS bounce (+12%) is encouraging, but we need to watch channel-specific sell-thru rates, quarterly margin progression vs. guidance (20-22% adj. EBITDA margin for FY25), SKU velocity on new launches and brand health metrics (search trends, social sentiment) to ensure the market's cyclical skepticism does not prove valid. If these leading indicators prove sluggish, or if legal or competitor-driven shocks re-ignite consumer pullback, Olaplex's recovery trajectory-and our above-consensus EPS estimates-could remain trapped under the sector's risk premium.

Conclusion

We see Olaplex as the most asymmetric call in specialty beauty, with Street models materially underestimating both the velocity and durability of an earnings rebound tied to accelerating premium sell-through and structural interest expense relief. Our conviction is backed by data-driven inflection across the board - from brand campaign momentum to margin recovery - and we see as unlocking leverage and alpha as the path to recovery remains at risk to consumer downgrading and competitive intensity. For sophisticated investors, we see an attractive entry point for a sharp earnings snapback, and recommend watching for specialty retail sell-through and margin trajectory to validate and catalyze the next leg of rerating.

data.title: CVR Partners Has Become The Bargain To Own In The Fertilizer Industry
data.description:
Introduction

CVR Partners (NYSE:UAN) is a limited partnership that covers the supply chain of nitrogen fertilizer products. It operates as a growth company in the agricultural chemicals industry with products such as ammonium nitrate and general ammonia. These are mainly used for crop yield in key states such as Texas, Nebraska, and others.

The company's stock has remained relatively flat since 2023, and with a current dividend yield of 8.6%, I want to determine if the company is worth investing in for income-focused investors.

Operation Overview

The company has two production sites, which are used to generate Ammonia and UAN. The company's facility in Kansas utilizes a petroleum coke gasification process, which is sourced from the main company, CVR Energy. The facility can produce around 1,300 Tpd of ammonia and 3,000 Tpd of UAN. The Illinois facility, on the other hand, uses mainly natural gas as feedstock for its hydrogen production, which diversifies feedstock risk from the Coffeyville facility. It is optimized for regional agricultural demand and achieved a 100% ammonia utilization rate. Both of these facilities underwent a successful turnaround, ensuring superior reliability and efficiency.

Market Dynamics

One of the primary drivers of CVR Partners' business would be the production from wheat farmers in the Midwest, in which it is heavily tied to planting seasons and crop prices. High grain prices have supported an increased planted acreage since 2023, which boosted the demand for nitrogen fertilizers. High commodity prices have pushed farmers to boost production and therefore boost CVR's revenues.

The industry has also grappled with oversupply following the price hike of 2022 due to the Russia-Ukraine war that caused worldwide geopolitical disruptions. Low-cost producers such as Russia and the Middle East flooded the market, further depressing prices, as we can see on CVR's revenue graph.

Looking at energy prices, the company has a competitive edge with its two different feedstock options that can hedge against rising gas prices. The use of Pet Coke from CVR Energy will protect the company from nat gas volatility. The slump in nat gas prices since 2022 has been making the East Dubirque facility more competitive cost-wise.

CVR is also facing tighter environmental regulations regarding emissions and waste. The growing demand for sustainable agriculture is currently pressuring fertilizer companies such as FMC to adopt greener products. This could put CVR Partners at risk of having more compliance costs, reducing its bottom line.

Valuation

The company currently has a fairly low P/E of 11.57, around 50% lower than the sector median. EV/EBITDA is also low at 6.82 compared to 9.74 for the sector. The company also has an impressive FCF generation with a current FCF yield of 14.3%, which is the reason why the company can pay such a high dividend yield to investors. Talking about the dividend, the company last paid a distribution of $2.26 last quarter, which results in an 8.6% yield, that has grown by 27.59% CAGR over the last 5 years. It is important to note that the dividend has not been steady over the years, with some quarters seeing a $10 distribution while some have had no distribution. The policy for the company is to distribute all available cash each quarter, which makes the distribution volatile depending on outside market conditions.

Growth metrics are poor across the board when looking at the 3-YR CAGR rate for the company and peers due to the exceptional conditions of 2022 that resulted in an incredible year for the industry. Top and bottom lines have normalized, but are still way higher than before the COVID Period for CVR Partners.

While looking at the charts, I've noticed a good trait while judging any investments: the lowest valuation metrics among peers for the strongest margin and returns metrics among them. The company has been able to climb back to the 40% level in gross margins following the 2017/2018 period. Furthermore, the company has strong capital efficiency, with the ROE at around 25% for the last period. This trend has been going on since 2020 with its 100% ammonia utilization rate and the successful turnarounds for its facilities outside of simple price increases.

Risks

CVR's revenue and bottom line are, at the end of the day, heavily dependent on the prices of UAN and Ammonia, which are in turn dependent on dynamics outside of CVR's control. Further production from low-cost producers could depress prices, reducing the company's revenues, although its coke facility in Kansas could help mitigate the risks of lower prices.

The company is also dependent on the overall agricultural activity of the country, more so in the Midwest, which could be impacted by weather conditions and planting decisions. If overall crop production gets reduced, the demand for CVR's products will slow as well.

There is, of course, the risk of natural gas spiking, even if it is mitigated by the coke supply from CVR Energy. If it increases, this will increase production costs as seen in 2022, which could squeeze margins and reduce its return metrics, thus reducing payouts to shareholders.

Conclusion

CVR Partners is a tricky investment to judge. I like the potential distribution positives, and I believe that the company is well-positioned to capture potential higher prices while also having some downside protection with its energy sourced from its parent company. At this particular time, I believe that the company is a buy, which should reward investors with its distribution while prices remain stable.

data.title: As The World Reprices, So Should Your Geographic Exposure
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

Something subtle but significant is underway in global equity markets. After years of U.S.-centric asset allocation dominance, 2025 is shaping up as a pivot point. Inflows into equity strategies that deliberately exclude U.S. exposure have reversed a multiyear trend of net outflows, with more than $2.5 billion added in just the last three months.1 This is not simply a temporary reaction to political volatility; it's an early recognition that the rest of the world is increasingly writing its own macroeconomic and corporate profitability stories. And for U.S.-based investors, the implications are timely: diversification is no longer a hedge—it may be a source of structural return.

Europe: From Stagnation Stereotype to Strategic Pivot

European equities have outperformed consensus expectations in 2025, and not just because of currency dynamics.2 Core sectors like banking and defense have benefited from a steepening yield curve, a surge in defense commitments and recovering earnings power. While gross domestic product (GDP) growth remains subdued, equity markets are reflecting something deeper: stock-level dispersion is unusually high, signaling a return to idiosyncratic alpha. Structurally, five forces are at play:3

  • Political will around the Savings and Investments Union, with progress on securitization deregulation expected by June 17, 2025.
  • Signs of artificial intelligence (AI) adoption delivering return on investment (ROI), with over 60% of the MSCI Europe Index weighted toward AI adopters.
  • Execution around Germany's fiscal expansion, including project awards likely in the fall and an unlimited defense budget.
  • Structurally rising defense spending, expected to ultimately pull in related tech investment.
  • Resilient mergers and acquisitions (M&A) activity, supported by easing competition rules, healthy balance sheets and a fragmented corporate landscape.

In valuation terms, European equities continue to trade at a meaningful discount to U.S. peers—yet the earnings base is strengthening beneath the surface.

Japan: Corporate Reform Moves from Narrative to Earnings

In Japan, what had been a hopeful reform story is increasingly showing up in the numbers. Domestic-oriented companies have outperformed exporters, bolstered by wage growth, better governance and targeted capital allocation improvements.4 While global investors have long debated the sustainability of Japan’s equity rallies, the current environment is different. The Tokyo Stock Exchange’s push for governance reform, combined with pressure to unwind legacy cross-holdings and simplify parent-subsidiary structures, is translating into better margin profiles and return on equity.5 Strategists see the potential for continued rerating as Japanese companies deliver earnings less sensitive to the yen and more tied to corporate behavior.

Portfolio Construction: Moving Beyond Home Bias

The rethink of geographic allocation is not simply about avoiding risks—it’s about capturing evolving structural asymmetries. While U.S. equities remain powerful innovation engines, their dominance in global indexes now masks the opportunity set elsewhere. Many institutional equity portfolios carry a hidden overexposure to U.S. mega-cap tech, and the question is not whether to abandon it, but whether the next decade's return profile will be better balanced with regions undergoing deep structural change.6 Europe and Japan are offering what U.S. equities delivered in past cycles: rerating potential linked to margin expansion, not just revenue growth.

The Stock Picker's Market Is Global Now

Perhaps most compelling is that beneath modest top-down GDP figures, international markets are offering highly fertile ground for active strategies. Stock-level dispersion in Europe is running above historical averages, and factors like idiosyncratic momentum, earnings revisions and accruals are delivering higher-than-normal Sharpe ratios. These are precisely the environments where stock selection—not sector or beta allocation—adds real value.7 For U.S.-based allocators, this is not about abandoning what has worked domestically but recognizing that the alpha is no longer confined by geography. The structural bull cases being built abroad—especially in sectors like energy infrastructure, financial modernization and dual-use technology—are too material to ignore.

The WisdomTree Dynamic International Equity Fund (DDWM)

DDWM is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the WisdomTree Dynamic International Equity Index. This broad approach (2,439 positions as of May 23, 2025) includes developed international dividend payers and weights them based on their cash dividends. Weighting in this way as opposed to market capitalization tends to mitigate the risk of undue exposure to firms with higher valuations.

Figure 1: Standardized Returns

Figure 2 highlights the consistent outperformance of the DDWM strategy over the MSCI EAFE Index across multiple timeframes, particularly over the past one, three and five years. While the MSCI EAFE leads year-to-date in 2025, DDWM has delivered higher compounded returns over the longer term, including since inception. This pattern points to a strategy that rewards patience and has the potential to compound effectively across cycles.

Figure 2: DDWM’s Long-Term Return Strength vs. MSCI EAFE Index

Figure 3 compares sector allocations between DDWM and the MSCI EAFE Index as of April 30, 2025. DDWM exhibits notable over-weight positions in Financials, Health Care and Utilities while under-weighting sectors like Information Technology and Real Estate compared to the benchmark. The allocation suggests an emphasis on quality and defensiveness, with a tilt toward income-generating and stable-growth sectors.

Figure 3: DDWM’s Sector Dispersion Is Not Extreme Compared to MSCI EAFE Index

Figure 4 highlights that DDWM delivers a higher total shareholder yield than the MSCI EAFE Index, driven by both dividend yield and share reduction yield. By weighting holdings based on cash dividends, DDWM naturally emphasizes companies with stronger income distribution practices, resulting in a 4.06% trailing 12-month dividend yield versus 3.02% for the benchmark. While share repurchases are not the primary selection factor, the portfolio still benefits from a higher 1.27% share reduction yield.

Figure 4: Total Shareholder Yield for International Equities

Figure 5 underscores DDWM’s relative valuation advantage across key metrics compared to the MSCI EAFE Index. Whether looking at price-to-earnings, price-to-cash flow or price-to-book, DDWM consistently trades at more attractive levels—helping mitigate valuation risk. For investors concerned about elevated multiples in U.S. markets, this signals a disciplined approach to international equity exposure with a strong value orientation.

Figure 5: DDWM’s Overall Lower Valuation vs. MSCI EAFE Index

Conclusion: A More Disciplined Path to Global Diversification

In a world where investors are increasingly questioning concentration risk and valuation extremes in U.S. equities, DDWM presents a differentiated, rules-based approach to international markets that blends quality, income and valuation discipline. Its emphasis on dynamic currency hedging, shareholder yield and lower valuation multiples delivers a portfolio designed to navigate global complexity without overpaying for growth. For those seeking to rebalance their equity exposure toward regions undergoing meaningful structural reform—while still demanding disciplined capital return and resilient fundamentals—DDWM stands out as a compelling vehicle to reengage with the broader opportunity set beyond U.S. borders.

Footnotes

1Source: “Investors flock to equity funds that exclude US,” Financial Times, 5/13/25.

2Source: “Europe Insight: European equities mid-year outlook – Navigating choppy waters,” Morgan Stanley, Morgan Stanley Research, 5/21/25.

3Source: Morgan Stanley, 2025.

4Source: “Japan Insight: Japan equity strategy mid-year outlook – Patience before triumph,” Morgan Stanley MUFG, Morgan Stanley Research, 5/20/25.

5Source: Morgan Stanley MUFG, 2025.

6Source: Morgan Stanley, 2025.

7Source: Morgan Stanley, 2025.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including possible loss of principal. Foreign investing involves special risks, such as risk of loss from currency fluctuation or political or economic uncertainty. The Fund invests in derivatives in seeking to obtain a dynamic currency hedge exposure. Derivative investments can be volatile, and these investments may be less liquid than other securities, and more sensitive to the effects of varied economic conditions. Derivatives used by the Fund may not perform as intended. A Fund that has exposure to one or more sectors may be more vulnerable to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. The composition of the Index underlying the Fund is heavily dependent on quantitative models and data from one or more third parties, and the Index may not perform as intended. The Fund invests in the securities included in, or representative of, its Index regardless of their investment merit, and the Fund does not attempt to outperform its Index or take defensive positions in declining markets. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Prior to April 30, 2025, the Fund was known as the WisdomTree Dynamic Currency Hedged International Equity Fund. The Fund’s ticker, investment objective and principal investment strategy remain unchanged.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Original Post

data.title: U.S. Labor Market 'Frozen' As Job Cuts, Hiring Largely Stall
data.description:
US job security is the most stable it has been in decades as policy and economic uncertainties have kept companies from imposing widespread staff cuts.

About 1.1% of employed Americans involuntarily left their jobs in April, a slight uptick from 1% in March and matching the average from the last 12 months, according to the latest US Bureau of Labor Statistics data released June 3. This post-COVID-19 rate of employers firing workers is significantly lower than the rate from the 20 years prior to the pandemic, the government data shows.

Firings and layoffs have failed to meaningfully rise despite lingering concerns of a coming recession, the Federal Reserve's plan to keep interest rates at current levels into late this year and President Donald Trump's push to impose sweeping tariffs that may lead to inflation.

Amid the current economic landscape, companies are not firing, but they are also not hiring and workers are staying put.

"The labor market is frozen in place," said Aaron Sojourner, a labor economist and senior researcher at the W.E. Upjohn Institute for Employment Research. "Huge uncertainty makes doing nothing appealing. No hiring. No firing."

From December 2000 to December 2019, the layoff and discharge rate averaged 1.4% and fell to 1.1% in only four months of the period. Over the past four years, the rate has been at 1.1% or lower in all but three months, or roughly 94% of the time, the data shows.

There were 1.79 million layoffs in the US in April, up about 200,000 from a year earlier and the highest since October 2024, according to the latest US Bureau of Labor Statistics data. The modest increase came as the US unemployment rate increased to 4.2% in April 2025 from 3.9% in April 2024.

"Layoffs remain very low by historical standards — lower than you would expect given the current unemployment rate," said Guy Berger, director of economic research at the Burning Glass Institute.

The hiring rate hit 3.5% in April, an uptick from March but near its lowest level in over four years.

Companies that had challenges hiring new employees in the wake of the pandemic are keeping workers on payroll now, said Nela Richardson, chief economist with ADP, during a June 4 call with reporters.

"That was a recent lesson learned: If you lay off workers, they may not come back," Richardson said.

In addition, companies are seeing relatively robust levels of productivity out of workers so there is no need to shed staffing numbers, Richardson said.

Job security may have been higher in the spring of 2023 when unemployment fell to 3.4%, its lowest level since the 1960s, and workers had more leverage to negotiate for substantially higher salaries and other incentives, such as the ability to work from home more, Richardson said.

But job security may be more stable than it has been in decades and could persist as long as the domestic economy avoids recession.

"As long as wages are robust, layoffs are low and consumers keep spending, I think even if the economy slows for a period of time, it is resilient enough to rebound," Richardson said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Broadcom: Limited Upside Ahead (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Executive Summary

Broadcom (NASDAQ:AVGO) released another stellar quarterly report just recently, which is a massive positive factor that supports my confidence in the bullish outlook. By the way, the previous STRONG BUY thesis aged very well because the stock returned a total of almost 40% to its investors since early March. The company's organic revenue is really impressive, the management's outlook regarding AI revenues is quite bullish, and Broadcom's profitability remains unparalleled. The stock did not soar after hours despite the strong earnings release, which is good as it means that investors still have a chance to buy the attractively valued AI superstar. On the other hand, this time DCF suggests that the upside is around 10%, which is not as attractive as the 44% which was calculated in my March analysis. Therefore, becoming slightly more prudent is highly likely to be sound, and I believe that AVGO is currently a BUY.

AVGO Q2 Earnings Review

Broadcom's Q2 earnings release was really impressive due to several reasons. The company's quarterly revenue reached the record level of $15 billion, indicating a 20% YoY growth. If we dig a little deeper, we find out that revenue growth was supported by strength in both of the company's core businesses: Semiconductor Solutions and Infrastructure Software. This indicates Broadcom's extremely strong revenue mix, which is nothing but a result of the management's visionary talent and adherence to the company's long-term growth strategy.

If we look at each segment separately, there are more reasons for optimism. Semiconductor Solutions growth was mostly fueled by AI semiconductor revenue, which was up 46% YoY, marking the staggering 9th consecutive quarter of aggressive growth. Such a strong growth over several quarters clearly indicates Broadcom's firm positioning in the AI revolution.

With such a strong positioning in the global AI infrastructure build out, we can expect AVGO's AI semiconductor revenue growth to remain strong over multiple quarters. I am confident in this forecast because cloud giants continue to pour billions into building and powering new data centers with fresh examples of Amazon, Google, and Oracle.

The fact that the Infrastructure Software segment's revenue delivered a strong 25% YoY growth is also extremely positive. First, it indicates that the quite expensive acquisition of VMWare was strategically sound as it started paying off. Second, the bold post-acquisition move to switch VMWare's business model to the VCF subscription-based approach also appears to be a correct strategic decision.

Broadcom's expanding profitability is another big sign, reinforcing my optimism. The adjusted EBITDA grew by 35% YoY, indicating the company's excellent operating leverage, as this metric's growth significantly outpaced sales growth. The company's Q2 free cash flow represented 43% of revenue, which is absolutely fantastic profitability, meaning that every $100 of sales is converted into $43 of pure shareholder value.

The management's Q3 guidance also looks promising as consolidated sales are projected to be $15.8 billion, which will be 21% higher compared on a YoY basis. This strength is once again expected to be powered by strength in both segments, which I like very much. The best part of the guidance, in my opinion, is that AVGO's management expects its AI revenue growth to accelerate to 60% YoY by reaching $5.1 billion.

The Upside Potential Is Modest

Broadcom's cost of equity is 8.85%. A quite high discount rate for this wide-moat company is primarily explained by the current risk-free rate. Therefore, the discount rate is likely to soften over time as the Fed returns to interest rates cuts. Nevertheless, we are still in the reality of high treasury yields, which I prefer to balance out by an optimistic growth rate into perpetuity. Moreover, the fundamentally strong AI champion like AVGO certainly deserves a 4% growth rate into perpetuity.

Consensus projections forecast the company's EPS to show robust growth over the next five years. With that being said, it will be fair enough to incorporate aggressive FCF growth assumptions into my DCF analysis.

The table with consensus EPS forecasts suggests growth from $6.64 in FY2025 to $13.47 in FY2029, which equals a CAGR of approximately 19.4%. This is a fairly conservative assumption for the DCF model due to AVGO's proven record of driving revenue and profitability growth. The first year FCF assumption is built on the FY2024 levered FCF of $28.315 billion multiplied by the projected (we can see above) FY2025 36.4% EPS growth. Therefore, the base year FCF is $36.621 billion.

AVGO's net present value is the sum of year 1-5 free cash flow PVs and the company's terminal value. The total fair value of the company is $1.33 trillion, which is 10% higher than the current market cap.

Potential Drawbacks

I have to say that this time the upside potential is much smaller compared to my previous AVGO thesis. I had more conviction last time because the stock was extremely cheap, as my DCF model suggested a 44% upside potential in March. Therefore, it is time to become slightly more cautious in my bullishness.

Moreover, AVGO gained almost 30% within just one last month, which is a sharp movement. This does not only indicate the stock's inherently high volatility (which is also a risk for short-term investors) but also increases the risks of investors taking profits, particularly in the current uncertain environment.

The geopolitical uncertainty and potential adverse developments around trade policy are still on the table. The market became quite greedy since the trade deal between the U.S. and China, but there were some hawkish statements from President Trump recently. Therefore, it seems that it might be too early for the stock market to become too greedy. New hawkish statements or tweets from Trump might trigger new panic waves for the stock market, which will also adversely affect the AVGO share price.

The Verdict

I think that after a massive 40% rally since my previous thesis, AVGO currently deserves a slight downgrade. Fundamentals look excellent, but I cannot say that 10% upside potential deserves the highest rating. Therefore, AVGO is a BUY at the moment (not a STRONG BUY).

data.title: Lindblad Expeditions Holdings, Inc.: Poor Fundamentals And Better Opportunities
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I've previously covered the cruise line industry on this platform where I identified Royal Caribbean Cruises Ltd. (RCL) as the all-around best in this sector in two articles over the last year. Lindblad Expeditions Holdings, Inc. (LIND) primarily operates small expedition ships that provide unique experiences to their guests. However, given the current fundamentals of Lindblad Expeditions Holdings, Inc., they are not in a strong financial position to reward shareholders in the near future. Further, when comparing LIND to successful industry peers, you can see a clear difference in returning shareholder value. Based on their poor fundamentals, I would rather invest in other industry peers if I had to choose an investment in the cruise line sector, even given their unique partnership with National Geographic.

Company Outlook And Earnings

Lindblad Expeditions Holdings, Inc. has been around for almost 50 years and offers small, unique cruise experiences to their guests and has a partnership with National Geographic. Currently, the cruise line operates 23 expeditions ships with capacity ranging from 16 to 148 guests and will have the ability to use National Geographic's brand until at least 2040. I believe this partnership puts LIND in a unique position in the cruising sector as they can offer more unique experiences to a niche market. However, as you will see with the fundamentals below, I'm not sure that this partnership will be enough to turn the company around.

Last quarter, LIND announced per share earnings of $0.03 and revenue of $179.72 million, which were both higher than analysts' expectations for the quarter. Management highlighted double-digit improvements in revenue and EBITDA while margins also improved 260 basis points. Ship occupancy still remains lower than pre-pandemic levels, however, they believe this should normalize in 2026. The company has targeted three main pillars for overall improvement, and these pillars will focus on maximizing revenue, improving costs, and exploring growth opportunities. It is reassuring that the company is piloting new on-board programs to drive future bookings, which will then drive growth, however, with their current fundamental picture I'm unsure of how successfully they will be able to expand through new growth opportunities while also trying to keep costs low. As you will see below, there is inconsistent performance on a yearly basis with their fundamentals and where expectations are for the company. For now, there seems to be more risk than reward for something I would invest in but, this was not a kitchen sink type of quarter, and it's nice to see some improvements in their occupancy levels.

Fundamentals

LIND has negative earnings per share on a yearly basis, so they do not have trailing-twelve-month or forward price/earnings ratio. LIND has a trailing twelve-month EV/EBITDA ratio of 13.24 and a forward EV/EBITDA 10.68. Comparing to industry peers, RCL has a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 13.56, Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings Ltd. (NCLH) has a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 8.4, and Carnival Corporation & plc (CCL) has a forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 8.7. When comparing these indicators, we can see that RCL is trading at a slight premium, which can be justified by their high growth, but LIND is also trading at a premium that is not justified by their fundamentals below. Further, LIND currently has a very high level of debt. According to the information on Seeking Alpha's valuation page, they have a higher level of debt than market capitalization which could be an indication that they may struggle to raise more capital to pay off this debt if they needed to in the future.

Revenue and Dilution

LIND has done a good job increasing their overall revenue faster than costs have increased, however, the company has diluted shareholders over this time. Since 2018, LIND has a negative share repurchase, tracked as the share's buyback ratio in the chart below, which has effectively diluted shareholders. The two largest negative buybacks were in 2019 and 2022; in 2019 there was a -9.2% share buyback ratio, and in 2022 it was -4.64%. Another concerning trend is that even though revenue has increased about 96% since 2019, costs have increased 114%. In a company that I'm invested in, I would like to see revenue growth outpace increases in costs. Also displayed in the chart below is their current interest rate on debt. The current rate of their debt is about 7.3%, which may be a problem if there is any concern about a slowing consumer in this sector.

Poor Returns on Investment

Another concern that I have with LIND is their poor return on invested capital [ROIC] compared to their weighted cost of capital [WACC]. As you can see in the chart below, although ROIC has improved recently from being negative during the pandemic, it is still well behind WACC which has not improved since 2020. Ideally, I look for companies that have a positive ROIC-WACC ratio for my investments, however, even RCL does not have a positive ratio at this time as discussed in my most recent coverage of the stock.

Looking at other fundamentals for LIND, we see an inconsistent picture on returns for assets, capital, capital employed, retained earnings, and incremental invested capital. As you can see below, LIND was significantly impacted by the pandemic, and as return on retained earnings [RORE] is the 5-year average, it is a lagging indicator that has now been impacted over the last year. So far, LIND has struggled to provide valuable returns for shareholders and the inconsistency present here is concerning. In future earnings, I would look for increases in returns on equity [ROE] that may start to indicate a turnaround in the fundamentals. Currently, they do not have ROE because they have negative equity, which is another indication that their debt is a problem that investors should pay attention to.

Comparison To RCL

Below, I have a graph depicting RCL's returns versus LINDs, and as you can see, there is a stark difference between the two companies. Granted, LIND operates smaller cruise vessels in a more unique space, however, shareholders are not receiving a return on their investment when purchasing shares of LIND over a peer in the sector like RCL. Further, as LIND will also expand into river cruises in the U.K., as they mentioned in their earnings call, they will be competing with RCL given that they are also expanding into river cruising, as I discussed in my last article. RCL is trending upwards on recovering from the pandemic entirely at this point, while LIND's fundamentals remain weak.

Per Share Value

Currently, LIND is struggling to produce value for shareholders. As you can see in the chart below, LIND has a high level of debt per share, negative book value per share, and has negative earnings per share. Although some metrics have improved since the pandemic lows, such as their cash per share, EBITDA per share, and revenue per share, LIND is still producing negative yearly earnings with a large amount of debt that they would not be able to pay off with the cash they have on hand. The biggest issue here is that they continue to produce inconsistent earnings, which we also saw prior to the pandemic. This is another fundamental that I see as an issue for the company, and it causes me to stay away from an investment here.

Fair Value

One positive that I found is that based on a discounted cash flow model where I used their trailing twelve-month free cash flow and one year total revenue growth as the estimated growth rate for the next 10 years, I came up with a fair value price of $11.81 per share. This assumes that the company will grow free cash flow at 9.2% for the next 10 years and grows in perpetuity starting in year 11 at 3%. I applied a 15% required rate of return as a margin of safety to the model, but with negative current free cash flow growth over the last year, this model may be optimistic. Another flaw to the model is that I did not use yearly earnings per share as it is currently negative, which is another concern that I have for the safety of LIND as an investment. I usually use a discounted cash flow model based on EPS as another measure in addition to the FCF to get a better picture of the company's ability to provide an investment return for my capital.

Risks To Thesis

The cruise line industry attracts a wide variety of consumers and has shown resilience over the last few years as we have come out of the pandemic lows. Although LIND currently displays poor fundamentals and has not yet fully recovered from the impact caused by the pandemic, it does not mean that they never will. LIND may be able to turn the fundamentals around as cruising remains popular among consumers, but I would rather invest into a better fundamental company with the current macroeconomic uncertainties. Further, even though I used RCL as a comparison, and they may be the best overall cruise line in this space, they are taking on a new business venture by expanding more into river tours which could be a little out of their area of expertise. This could impact the fundamentals of the better performing peer in this sector and cause impacts to other companies. Another potential risk for this negative thesis on LIND is a buyout rather than letting the company deteriorate entirely. If larger cruise lines, such as RCL, are looking to rapidly expand into unique experiences, LIND could be an acquisition target given that they offer unique experiences to small cohorts of people through their unique partnerships rather than the larger crowds hosted on the main cruise lines now, and this could drive the price of the stock up.

Conclusion

In conclusion, I believe LIND will continue to struggle in the future and that there's likely better places for capital allocation. LIND's ROIC-WACC ratio remains significantly negative, their effective interest rate on debt is high, book value has declined over the last few years, and earnings per share are currently negative. Even though LIND has negative key fundamentals, they still trade at a premium compared to the industry when looking at their EV/EBITDA ratio. Given the negative fundamentals LIND has displayed over the last few years, I believe there is more risk in an investment into LIND than other peers in the cruise line industry, which have a better overall fundamental picture. While peers in this space have already recovered from the pandemic, LIND continues to struggle and has not provided a great return to shareholders. This article is based on my personal opinions and research about the company and investors should do their own due diligence before making financial decisions.

Appendix

Below, I've also included a graph with LIND's margins, which indicates gross margins have recovered to pre-pandemic levels at this time, but other margins remain inconsistent. With net margins still negative, I'm waiting to see if there's a change to positive net margins before reconsidering.

data.title: Yippy Yields And Dollar Dilemmas
data.description:
By Agatha Malinowski

Equity markets have made a remarkable recovery following a turbulent start to the second quarter, with the S&P 500® closing out May up 6%. However, ongoing uncertainties surrounding tariffs and their inflationary impact, concerns about the fiscal deficit and potential shifts in foreign demand for U.S. Treasuries have weighed on the bond market, with the iBoxx $ Treasuries down 1% in May — marking its first monthly loss this year.

Despite declines across the yield curve on June 4, 2025, following a soft labor market report, 2-Year and 30-Year U.S. Treasury yields have surged by over 23 and 27 bps,1 respectively, since the end of April, with the 30-Year Treasury yield briefly surpassing 5% following weak demand in the 20-Year U.S. Treasury auction. As a result, the spread between short- and long-term U.S. Treasury yields has widened, suggesting that investors may be demanding greater compensation for holding longer-term debt. Exhibit 1 highlights the spread between 2-Year and 30-Year U.S. Treasury yields, which has increased to over 100 bps, reaching levels not seen since 2022.

Rising yields also may affect the potential for diversification across asset classes. Traditionally, bonds have tended to exhibit an inverse correlation to equities, with notable outliers such as most of 2024. However, after witnessing negative correlations so far this year, Exhibit 2 reveals that the six-month correlations between The 500™ and the S&P U.S. Treasury Bond Current 30-Year Index have turned positive. If sustained, a positive relationship could be indicative of a potential headwind for risk-adjusted performance for combinations of both asset classes.

In addition to their impact on diversification, we can also examine the currency dynamics associated with an increase in bond yields, which could be particularly relevant for global asset owners who may be affected by currency hedging decisions. Notably, despite the rise in long-term U.S. Treasury yields, the U.S. dollar has weakened, with the S&P U.S. Dollar Futures Index down 4% quarter-to-date. Although the dollar stabilized in May, the currency’s weakness coinciding with rising bonds yields marks a departure from their typical positive relationship, as shown in Exhibit 3. Recent risks, including fiscal concerns and doubts about the dollar’s safe haven status, may have contributed to this diverging trend.

While the future trajectory of U.S. Treasury yields remains uncertain, we can look to market expectations for long-dated U.S. Treasury volatility, as measured by the options market with the Cboe 20+ Year Treasury Bond ETF Volatility Index (VXTLT). After declining from the highs observed during the market turbulence in April, Exhibit 4 shows that VXTLT rose along with longer-dated yields, surpassing the 20 mark on May 21, 2025, and closed above the 17 handle as of June 4.

Looking ahead, although numerous unknowns remain, the impact of U.S. Treasury yields on the term premium, along with the associated diversification and currency dynamics, could have significant implications for asset owners across asset classes.

1 Data as of June 4, 2025.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Centene: Strong Execution And Margin Discipline Support Potential Upside
data.description:
Centene (CNC) is one of those companies in the medical services market that is overlooked in my opinion. This medical giant manages the largest portfolio of Medicaid policies in the country, dominates the ACA insurance exchanges, and leads the Medicare Part D drug coverage market, serving nearly 29 million insured people as of 2024 end. That is why it is definitely worth taking a closer look at the essence of Centene's business, delving into its finances, and trying to assess the fair value of the shares.

From Medicaid Leader To Diversified Insurer

Centene's business model is quite simple: the company sells medical policies under contracts with the US government and just to private clients. After signing a contract with the state, the company receives a fixed premium from the states in which it operates or the federal government, and in exchange undertakes to cover the medical expenses of program participants. In order for the profit to remain in this scheme, Centene must keep payments to participants below the bonuses they receive. Accordingly, the more insured people there are, the more profitable the business is. There are now more than 29 million such insured people. It turns out that the key for the company is the ability to effectively control costs and act systematically: managing drug programs, prevention, and reducing repeat hospitalizations. Additionally, Centene can receive bonuses from Medicare for high-quality ratings.

More than half of the revenue in 2024 came from the Medicaid program ($83.8 billion, 57.6%), followed by the Commercial segment ($33.7 billion, 23.2%), Medicare ($23 billion, 15.8%) and the rest ($4.9 billion, 3.4%). The growth rate of the largest segment is slowing down due to the fact that after the pandemic, the US government decided to conduct an audit of insured persons and some of the program participants were denied access to Medicaid. Centene's management expects tariff increases in 2025-2026 to offset costs. The Medicare segment is actively growing, especially in PDP, where Centene is the de facto market leader. In the first quarter of 2025, PDP membership grew by 22%. Marketplace is also not slowing down: almost 2 million new members were added in just one quarter.

What Centene's management has succeeded in, and for which the management can be praised, is the transition from a Medicaid-focused business model to a diversified multi-format insurer. The purchase of WellCare in 2020 also helped, which boosted Medicare revenues. And getting rid of non-core assets, such as Circle Health, allowed us to focus.

The market in which Centene operates is huge, with an estimated value of $3.5 trillion in 2024, but it is also very competitive. And she has strong competitors. In Medicaid — Elevation, UnitedHealth, Molina, CVS/Aetna. In Medicare Advantage — United Healthcare (UNH), Humana (HUM), and CVS Health (CVS). In Part D, Centene is the leader. Centene is among the largest in the Marketplace, especially with the Ambetter brand. But each of the competitors is strong in its segment, and the struggle is for shares, for customer retention, and for margins.

Strong Results For 1Q 2025

Centene reported well for the first quarter, showing significant revenue growth. Total revenue was $48.6 billion, with consensus expectations of $42.78 billion. The number of insured people on the Marketplace increased by 29% or 1.9 million new members. Similarly, in Medicare Part D, the increase in participants was +22%. In the Medicaid segment, the number of participants remained approximately stable (about 12.9–13.0 million), while the company managed to get about a +4% increase in rates, taking into account individual tariff updates. The commercial segment also showed significant growth due to Marketplace (ACA plans) and a small increase in corporate customers.

Centene's operating profit increased due to business scaling and a reduction in the share of management costs. EBIT was about $1.53 billion compared to $1.1 billion a year earlier (+39%). The EBIT margin was about 3.6% (compared to 3.0% in Q1 2024). The main reason for the improvement was reduced SG&A expenses relative to revenue: the SG&A ratio fell to 7.9% in the quarter (compared to 8.7% in Q1 2024), due to savings on administrative costs and economies of scale. D&A accounted for about 0.75% of revenue, so the EBITDA margin also remained at about 4-4.5%. In total, due to faster revenue growth compared to costs, the operating margin increased. Centene's net profit was $1.3 billion, compared to $1.163 million a year earlier. Diluted EPS increased from $2.16 to $2.63 (+22%). The net income margin remained unchanged at about 2.8–3.0% in the quarter, reflecting proportional growth in profit and revenue. Operational improvements were the main factor in net profit growth; interest expenses decreased slightly – by $8 million to $170 million – and the tax burden increased (the effective rate in the quarter was 24.7%).

Based on the strong start to the year and the current dynamics, Centene management has adjusted its forecasts for 2025. The main change is an increase in the revenue forecast for Premium & Service to the range of $164-166 billion (previously estimated at $158-160 billion). A $6 billion increase from the initial forecast was mainly due to additional revenue of $5 billion from Marketplace and stronger-than-expected results in Medicare Advantage ($1 billion). At the same time, the management did not change the forecast for adjusted EPS – it is still expected to be above $7.25. The CFO of Centene outlined three key factors underlying the forecast for 2025. First, a total increase of 4% is expected in the Medicaid segment, which will help offset the increase in medical costs. Secondly, in Medicare Advantage, the company adheres to the "inverted slope" strategy — low HBR at the beginning of the year, growth by the end, but maintaining annual margins. Third, despite the increased costs associated with the expansion of the network, Centene expects to improve operational efficiency and achieve its stated financial goals.

In my opinion, management's forecasts for 2025 look reasonable in the baseline scenario, but their implementation directly depends on the external environment, which the company only partially controls. The expected 4% increase in Medicaid rates may not cover the ongoing pressure on HBR, especially if states are slow to review and update medical rates — that is, they are delayed in responding to rising costs. The forecast for continued profitability in the Medicare Advantage segment also raises questions: a strategy with low medical costs at the beginning of the year and their growth by the end may lead to stronger margin fluctuations than management currently anticipates, especially against the background of changes in program rules after the IRA reform. Additionally, the retention or growth of the number of insured persons in the context of redetermination in Medicaid and increased competition in the Marketplace is a factor with a high level of uncertainty.

FCF Analysis And Valuation

Centene shares have been falling in value over the past year for several reasons: a decrease in the number of insured in Medicaid, rising medical costs [HBR], increased competition in Medicare Advantage, and market concerns about regulatory and tariff risks amid political uncertainty. The stock is already trading more than 20% cheaper than it was a year ago, and I think this is a good chance to take advantage of the moment and buy the oversold shares.

I built a DCF model with a forecast of UFCF up to 2030 in order to identify the fair value of Centene shares. The results of the UFCF calculations can be seen in the table below.

In my model, the cash flow forecast for Centene is based on the following key assumptions:

  • Revenue: I expect Centene's revenue to grow significantly in 2025, from $147.2 billion in 2024 to $168.1 billion in 2025 (an increase of +14.2%). Such a sharp increase is due to the fact that in Q1, the company significantly exceeded initial expectations for membership in the Obamacare and Medicare market (which was reflected in the increase in revenue forecast). After 2025, the growth rate will slow down to 5-8%. This scenario corresponds to a combination of the following factors: a sharp initial increase in the membership base (after the success of the marketplace and drug plans) followed by market consolidation. The revenue CAGR for the period 2025-2030 will be about 6.6%. In fact, this dynamic suggests that most of the additional participants have already been "captured" by 2025, and further growth is based on more moderate rates and insignificant organic gains.
  • EBIT: I predict that operating profit will grow approximately in proportion to revenue, while maintaining an operating margin of about 3.3–3.4%. Despite the increase in medical costs, I assume that due to savings on SG&A (which can already be seen from the latest results: SG&A's share of revenue dropped to 7.9% in Q1) and a decrease in HBR on average across groups (plus economies of scale), the operating margin will remain. Thus, the model assumes that Centene will maintain a high degree of operational efficiency in the coming years: additional revenues go almost directly to profit, while the total operating margin remains stable.
  • CapEx: In the model, I have included a very low level of capital investment for Centene – about 0.8–1.0% of revenue per year. This reflects the specifics of the industry: insurance companies do not need large investments in fixed assets. Centene spends most of its expenses on IT, mergers and acquisitions, and compliance with regulations – this scales with the business, but remains a small share of revenue. The forecast is focused on maintaining the current IT infrastructure and adding new systems (for example, PBM integration after the transition to CVS, improved analytics) - all this requires capital, but within about 1% of revenue annually.

Thus, according to my forecast, FCF will stay in the range of 1-2% of total revenue, which is actually very conservative, since the average FCF yield for the period in 2020-2024 was about 3.88%, and in some years it even reached 7%. But at the same time, I did not try to specifically underestimate FCF for excessive conservatism, but simply objectively assessed the future and the risks that Centene's business might face.

Further, when calculating the terminal cost, I use three approaches: the P/E multiplier method, the EV/EBITDA method and the perpetuity growth method. This allows you to estimate the range of possible fair value figures, depending on the selected parameters and the level of the discount rate. I got the WACC at 5%, which is quite low and is primarily due to the company's low five-year beta, which was about 0.46x. Plus, the Cost of debt was only 3.8%.
