I use the latest TTM Interest Expense divided by the latest one-year quarterly average debt to get the simplified cost of debt. The company's debt burden is small, and interest expenses are correspondingly low, so these results come out. In any case, I used several WACC values to simulate the stock price under different scenarios.

The consensus expects a P/E of 7.61x for 2025, so I use three scenarios in my calculations: a low P/E of 6x, a baseline of 7x, and an optimistic P/E of 8x. In the baseline scenario, with a WACC of 5.0% and a P/E of about 7x, the fair share price will be about $63 with an upside of 13%. In the worst-case scenario, when the WACC is 1% higher and the P/E is even lower (6x), a downside of up to 5% is formed. Thus, with the most positive inputs, the share price will be 31% higher than the current one ($55.4), and with the most negative ones, it will be 5% lower.

The situation is similar with the EV/EBITDA multiplier. Here, in the base scenario (WACC – 5%, EV/EBITDA – 6x), the fair price will be about $59 with a fairly limited upside of 7%. At the same time, with the most conservative input data, the price may fall by 13%, and with the most positive, it may rise by 27%.

The share price sensitivity table calculated using the Perpetuity Growth Method shows us an even more limited price upside of 6% in the baseline scenario, and in the worst-case scenario, if the WACC increases to 6% and the growth rate in the terminal period is about 2%, then the fair value of the share will be 7% lower than the current one. A positive scenario gives an upside of 34%.

I took the baseline scenario for each valuation method and calculated the average price among all three methods and got a fair value of the company's shares at $60 with an upside of about 9%. In a negative scenario, the downside is limited to 9%, and in an optimistic scenario, the price may rise by 31% to $72. At the same time, I consider my estimate to be quite conservative, especially in terms of calculating FCF. I assign a "Buy" rating to Centene.

Capital Return To Shareholders

Centene traditionally prefers to return capital to shareholders through share buybacks rather than through dividends. In recent years, it has been necessary to significantly stimulate the return of funds to investors. The board authorized a $4 billion increase to the existing share repurchase program. This was announced alongside raising Centene's 2024 outlook and reaffirming its 2023 guidance. In total, Centene bought about $7.6 billion of its shares in 2022-2024. The year 2024 was particularly active: the report for the 4th quarter of 2024 indicated that the company had bought 42.0 million shares worth $3.0 billion. This made it possible to reduce the number of shares in circulation and increase EPS figures. As of February 2025, there was about $2.2 billion of unused buyback limit, which the company can use if necessary. The terms of the program allow for flexible purchase of shares on the market, and Centene declares the right to suspend buybacks at any time. Thus, the current policy of returning funds to shareholders is through aggressive share buybacks.

Risks

In fact, working with the government creates both advantages and risks, so I would mark government regulation as the first risk. The slightest changes to the rules of Medicaid or Medicare can seriously affect revenue. For example, if states decide to cut funding or increase program requirements, Centene could quickly lose thousands of customers. The introduction of import tariffs by Trump creates pressure on the prices of medical equipment and, consequently, on business margins. You can also add a government review of citizens' rights to health programs, in particular to Medicaid, which led to a reduction in the number of insured persons. The result is already visible in the first quarter of 2025, when the total number of participants decreased by 1.7% year-on-year. At the same time, expenses are growing: the payout ratio (HBR) for Medicaid and commercial segments exceeded 89-93%, which practically reduces profits to zero. If insurance premiums don't rise faster than costs, Centene will be in a difficult position. All this is happening against the backdrop of increasing competition — giants like UnitedHealth and Humana are active in the same segments and are ready to win back market share. Additionally, operational and economic risks create pressure. Calculation failures, changes in CMS requirements, inflation, and high-interest rates can all affect business margins. Centene tries to hedge these threats, but the nature of its business itself implies a high sensitivity to the external environment.

Final Thoughts

In the current environment, Centene remains a systemically important player in the U.S. government health program market with strong positions in Medicaid, Medicare, and ACA marketplaces. The financial results for the first quarter of 2025 turned out to be strong, which allows us to look positively at the revenue growth potential in the current year. However, the implementation of the forecast depends on a number of factors beyond the company's control, from tariff flexibility at the state level to policy decisions in healthcare and inflationary pressures. My assessment shows that under basic assumptions, the fair value of the stock is $60 with an upside of about 9%, and the risk/return profile looks balanced. Given the current oversold securities, margin stabilization and the availability of a buyback program, Centene deserves a Buy rating in my opinion.
